summary: Gruber previews Apple's September 2014 event, predicting two new iPhones at 4.7 and 5.5 inches with identical internals but different display resolutions (@2x and @3x retina), with the larger model likely in short supply at launch. He argues the 5.5-inch model exists because people want huge phones and because larger batteries enable both better battery life and the power-hungry higher-resolution display. He explains how legacy apps will scale on the new screens and speculates extensively about Apple's wrist wearable, predicting it will defy the skeuomorphic smartwatch mold and do something fundamentally different from Android Wear. He expects the wearable to be controversial and possibly misunderstood by those who can only judge it against existing product categories.
key_points: The 5.5-inch iPhone's larger battery both justifies the @3x retina display (which draws ~20% more power) and delivers superior battery life, while the 4.7-inch model stays at @2x to preserve acceptable battery performance
The iPhone 5C's true purpose was creating a plastic iPhone that could eventually occupy the 'free' tier, and Apple may continue this strategy with a '5CS' combining plastic design with 5S internals
Legacy apps will run scaled/zoomed on the new displays rather than letterboxed, since the aspect ratio is unchanged and slight blurriness beats black bars on all sides
Apple's wearable will deliberately reject the skeuomorphic analog-watch approach that competitors like Moto 360 embrace, consistent with Apple's iOS 7/8 design direction
The wearable must excel at something entirely new — it won't succeed as merely a phone on your wrist or a traditional watch, because excellent phones and watches already exist
money_quotes: I suspect now the whole point of the iPhone 5C was to get to this point, where the plastic iPhone and the 'free' iPhone are one and the same.
Some people want to buy them. It doesn't matter why.
There are going to be people who will buy the 5.5-inch iPhone despite the size of the device, not because of it, just because of the battery life.
Plus, people who just spent hundreds of dollars on a huge new iPhone don't want to see tiny apps.
That sort of mimicry of real-world analog objects is exactly what Apple has just spent the last two years eliminating in iOS and OS X.
For the Apple watch to be worth creating, it must be excellent at something else.
Apple's watch won't fit in an existing mold. It won't be a phone on your wrist. It won't be a watch as we know it.
Perhaps even if they use the word 'watch', it may no more be a watch in the traditional sense of the word than the iPhone is a phone in the traditional sense.
key_insight: Apple's expansion to two iPhone sizes reflects market reality that one size doesn't fit all, while its wearable must transcend existing product categories rather than imitate them to justify its existence.
